Greek-Entry.English-Entry: δὶς] δεκάδας δ" ἐτέων πλήσας κεῖμαι τῶδ" ὑπὸ ὤβω
φάλαγγος τὸ ἔσχατον καὶ τὸ ἄκρον
to have the pains of childbirth be in travail
that which is born amid throes child
the cup passed round when a scolion was sung
push away from oneself force back
push against one another jostle struggle
the long quill-feathers in a wing
Ἀριστοφάνης ὀλέκρανα λέγει τὰ τῶν ὠλενῶν κρανία
the arm from the elbow downwards
because the priests who beat it were eunuchs
on the shoulder on the shoulders
scarred over too soon without healing properly
of the colour of the fresh vine-leaf
to be proud of broad shoulders
the shoulder with the upper arm
pickled flesh of the tunny"s shoulder
with one"s sleep not slept out
a prop for the forks of vines
bid for purchase the farming of public taxes
buy off avert by giving hush-money
contract for the farming of taxes
to be bought that may be bought
ὠνητής ὀπωρῶν ὠνήτωρ οἱ ἀγοραῖοι σὺ δὲ ὀπωρώνης λέγε
inspection of eggs divination from them
τοὺς Ἴωνας λέγειν φασὶ τὴν κωλῆν ὥρην καὶ ὡραίαν
κωλῆ λάψεται . . κωλῆν ἀντὶ τῆς ὤρης
the spring-time of life the bloom of youth
produce of the season fruits of the year
in the prime of life youthful
the ripeness of the fruits of the year
κατὰ τὴν εἰθισμένην ὥραν ἑκάστου ἔτους ἀναπολεῖν
making a din on the mountains
one who brings on the seasons
who gives the ripe fruits in their season
ὡρέοντα: χρονίζοντα ὧρος γὰρ ὁ χρόνος καὶ ὡρογράφοι οἱ περὶ χρόνου γράψαντες
ὠρεῖν: φυλάττειν ὅθεν καὶ ὁ θυρωρὸς λέγεται
bringing on the fruits in their season
who preside over the several hours of the day
to be in the ascendant at the natal hour
an instrument for telling the time a dial
for dividing and marking the hours
mark by its rising the time
fostering the seasons bringing them on
as it were so to speak
to push and be pushed about
ὠσχοί: τὰ νέα κλήματα σὺν αὐτοῖς τοῖς βότρυσιν
κλῆμα βότρυας φέρον ὀργῶντας καὶ γενναίους καὶ ὀσχοφόρια
τὰ τῆς ἀμπέλου κλήματα περιέχοντα βότρυας παρὰ Ἀριστοφάνει
the young men who carried the vine-branches
the mark of a wound scar
an instrument for laying hold of the ears
with ears as large as a winnowing-fan;
having a running from the ears
gain made in war spoil booty
to be of service to benefit
